issues.title: ABNF trouble
issues.author: chrysn
issues.body:
The ABNF provided in the appendix fails the test at https://author-tools.ietf.org/abnf, but inside code that came from https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8610#appendix-B

ABNF from -01:

```
cddl = S *(rule S)
rule = typename [genericparm] S assignt S type
     / groupname [genericparm] S assigng S grpent

typename = id
groupname = id

assignt = "=" / "/="
assigng = "=" / "//="

genericparm = "<" S id S *("," S id S ) ">"
genericarg = "<" S type1 S *("," S type1 S ) ">"

type = type1 *(S "/" S type1)

type1 = type2 [S (rangeop / ctlop) S type2]
; space may be needed before the operator if type2 ends in a name

type2 = value
      / typename [genericarg]
      / "(" S type S ")"
      / "{" S group S "}"
      / "[" S group S "]"
      / "~" S typename [genericarg]
      / "&" S "(" S group S ")"
      / "&" S groupname [genericarg]
      / "#" "6" ["." tag-number] "(" S type S ")"
      / "#" DIGIT ["." uint]                ; major/ai
      / "#"                                 ; any
tag-number = uint / ("<" type ">")


rangeop = "..." / ".."

ctlop = "." id

group = grpchoice *(S "//" S grpchoice)

grpchoice = *(grpent optcom)

grpent = [occur S] [memberkey S] type
       / [occur S] groupname [genericarg]  ; preempted by above
       / [occur S] "(" S group S ")"

memberkey = type1 S ["^" S] "=>"
          / bareword S ":"
          / value S ":"

bareword = id

optcom = S ["," S]

occur = [uint] "*" [uint]
      / "+"
      / "?"

uint = DIGIT1 *DIGIT
     / "0x" 1*HEXDIG
     / "0b" 1*BINDIG
     / "0"

value = number
      / text
      / bytes

int = ["-"] uint

; This is a float if it has fraction or exponent; int otherwise
number = hexfloat / (int ["." fraction] ["e" exponent ])
hexfloat = ["-"] "0x" 1*HEXDIG ["." 1*HEXDIG] "p" exponent
fraction = 1*DIGIT
exponent = ["+"/"-"] 1*DIGIT

text = %x22 *SCHAR %x22
SCHAR = %x20-21 / %x23-5B / %x5D-7E / %x80-10FFFD / SESC

SESC = "\" ( %x22 / "/" / "\" /                 ; \" \/ \\
             %x62 / %x66 / %x6E / %x72 / %x74 / ; \b \f \n \r \t
             (%x75 hexchar) )                   ; \uXXXX
hexchar = non-surrogate / (high-surrogate "\" %x75 low-surrogate)
non-surrogate = ((DIGIT / "A"/"B"/"C" / "E"/"F") 3HEXDIG) /
                ("D" %x30-37 2HEXDIG )
high-surrogate = "D" ("8"/"9"/"A"/"B") 2HEXDIG
low-surrogate = "D" ("C"/"D"/"E"/"F") 2HEXDIG

bytes = [bsqual] %x27 *BCHAR %x27
BCHAR = %x20-26 / %x28-5B / %x5D-10FFFD / SESC / "\'" / CRLF
bsqual = "h" / "b64"

id = EALPHA *(*("-" / ".") (EALPHA / DIGIT))
ALPHA = %x41-5A / %x61-7A
EALPHA = ALPHA / "@" / "_" / "$"
DIGIT = %x30-39
DIGIT1 = %x31-39
HEXDIG = DIGIT / "A" / "B" / "C" / "D" / "E" / "F"
BINDIG = %x30-31

S = *WS
WS = SP / NL
SP = %x20
NL = COMMENT / CRLF
COMMENT = ";" *PCHAR CRLF
PCHAR = %x20-7E / %x80-10FFFD
CRLF = %x0A / %x0D.0A
```

Error message:

```
(103:21): error: state 54, token "end of file": syntax error
parsing failed: 1 errors encountered
```

That's in the last two lines -- but those are identical to those in 8610... any ideas what's going wrong?

comments.author: cabo
comments.body: Weird.  Works for me.  Do you copy/paste into the form on https://author-tools.ietf.org/abnf ?

comments.author: cabo
comments.body: Ha.  Can reproduce if I delete the final newline.

issues.title: ABNF: CDDLism
issues.author: chrysn
issues.body:
While the summarized ABNF is OK (if one adds a final newline), the snippet in 3.2 does not validate even with the proper surroundings.

It says

```
type2 /= "#" "6" ["." tag-number] "(" S type S ")"
```

which uses a CDDL-style later alternative, whereas [ABNF's incremental alternatives](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5234.html#section-3.3) are `=/`.

Neither validates in https://author-tools.ietf.org/abnf, but that's probably OK because those are rarely used.

comments.author: cabo
comments.body:
Right, these snippets are extracted from the long

```
type2 = value
      / typename [genericarg]
      / "(" S type S ")"
      / "{" S group S "}"
      / "[" S group S "]"
      / "~" S typename [genericarg]
      / "&" S "(" S group S ")"
      / "&" S groupname [genericarg]
      / "#" "6" ["." tag-number] "(" S type S ")"
      / "#" DIGIT ["." uint]                ; major/ai
      / "#"                                 ; any
tag-number = uint / ("<" type ">")
```

So the question would be how best to phrase these extracts (certainly not with broken ABNF).

comments.author: chrysn
comments.body: The `=/` mechanism in ABNF's incremental alternatives does seem suitable. It's apparently a niche thing in ABNF (as evidenced by lack of support in the tool), but it's sufficient to create a small example that is valid ABNF.

comments.author: cabo
comments.body:
The  ABNF tool does support =/, but it requires type2 to already be defined (which is not needed in CDDL, by the way).
So the snippet would not validate stand-alone.

comments.author: chrysn
comments.body: Does it need to? It's common in documents to incrementally build ABNF, and to what I would assume to be Knuth's discontent, we do not have formal language that expresses order or dependencies. This snippet will need to be appended to the original RFC8610 ABNF to be valid.

comments.author: cabo
comments.body:
Well, these are snippets, not code that actually integrates with the existing ABNF.

(We do have a syntax for building models from snippets based on name=, but that is not used here, as we don't have the other snippets.)

comments.author: chrysn
comments.body:
To me, #4 is good enough to close this.

(as for don't have ... if only we could just import from another document ;-) )

issues.title: Have other errata been considered?
issues.author: chrysn
issues.body:
From [the check list](https://authors.ietf.org/required-content#summary-of-changes):

> Ensure any errata against RFCs that are being obsoleted or updated have been considered, and that the I-D clearly identifies those that have been addressed.

* [x] https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid6526 was about escaping in text, doesn't apply here
* [x] https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid6278 was about the role of the DELETE character, 0x7F. It's still allowed in BCHAR in the update -- are we still good at "the proper fix is not straightforward, and that it should be handled in the next version of the spec.", and why is this not the document to fix this in?
  (AIU this doesn't need to be stated in the document; an acknowledgement that we still don't know how to best address this in here suffices to me.)
* [x] https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid6527 is just done here
* [x] https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid6543 is resolved (not as suggested in the erratum, but resolved)
* [x] https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid6575 is not about the grammar, and this document updates the grammar, so nothing to be done.

comments.author: cabo
comments.body: [b7ef215](https://github.com/cbor-wg/update-8610-grammar/pull/6/commits/b7ef2154924683207a1f87b795327a506b9ad7f5) is intended to start to close #5

pulls.title: Add Unicode scalar hex escapes (\u{1F913} for 🤓) to SESC syntax
pulls.author: cabo

comments.body: I don't like this backslash syntax, but it looks like it's always been there.

We copied that from JSON...
(Not sure what specifically you don't like.)
Sorry, I get it now.  The %75 bit obfuscated it slightly.  It's fine as-is.

pulls.title: Add #7.<x> syntax for simple values.
pulls.author: cabo
pulls.body: As implemented in the tools cddl (0.11.1) and cddlc (0.3.1),

reviews.author: chrysn
reviews.body: Nice and simple on the grammar side. LGTM.

comments.body:
Does this need to include `uint` even? There is already a line in the type2 that is being extended that says ` / "#" DIGIT ["." uint]  ; major/ai` -- wouldn't that match first anyway, and thus no uint ever comes "down" this far?

(Not new for `#7`)

The sequence in the collected ABNF in Appendix A is

``` abnf
      / "#" "6" ["." head-number] "(" S type S ")"
      / "#" "7" ["." head-number]
      / "#" DIGIT ["." uint]                ; major/ai
```

So, in a PEG parser, the second line wins over the third line.
In a traditional parser generator, this might be an ambiguity; ABNF does not require avoiding ambiguities though.
